transcript.sec: Why Companies Fail Despite Great Talent

turns.en: Let's maybe use a counterfactual. Why do companies fail when they have great talent and they have, you know, seemingly all the same components that an applied intuition have? It's because maybe the best ideas are not being surfaced and certainly maybe they're not actually being adopted. Or more often than not when I think about companies that have been very successful is they have momentum going in a specific direction. And that momentum over that wall of sound overwhelms any new sound that's emerging which is hey, the market's changing. The market's changing. You just can't even hear that because there's all this momentum going in a particular direction. A good example I had front row seats for this when I worked at Google. It was the era where Facebook was emerging. And here was Google and people don't remember Google in the late double O's and early teens. Google wasn't just a company. It was the apex predator of Silicon Valley. Apple was just, you know, the MacBook Air had just come out. Steve Jobs was getting in the right direction but nothing like Apple is today. Amazon AWS was still a young thing. Nvidia was teetering off of bankruptcy. I mean, all these giant companies that you think of, Microsoft was run by Ballmer, you know, Twitter was a small thing. And then you So, it wasn't it was But Google was this already larger than life number one company. Everybody wanted to work at Google. And there were not many companies with that stature. And then in the periphery, this little company Facebook starts emerging. And Google, who has the best engineers on the planet, is making a billion in cash flow a month, tries to fight this little company. And I remember Facebook at that time maybe had a thousand people. And Google was like, you know, 15x, 20x the size with a lot of cash flow. And why couldn't Google fight Facebook? It's because Google is not Facebook. It's like that Confucian saying, like, how does a gorilla learn how to fly? By not being a gorilla. The way that Google would have won the social media wars by being a social media company. It's just fundamentally not. And so, the large This happens in companies all the time, which is you're just going in one direction with momentum, consciously or unconsciously, because that's what all the employees are, that's what the culture is, that's what they work on. And then something changes in the market and you just can't even move there. And I think that also can happen surprisingly at really small companies. So, where founders have a view and it's like that view is the view it's going to be.
turns.zh: 让我们用一个反事实的例子。为什么公司拥有优秀的人才和看似所有与 Applied Intuition 相同的要素却会失败？可能是因为最好的想法没有被提出来，当然也可能没有被采纳。或者，更常见的是，我认为非常成功的公司往往在一个特定方向上拥有 momentum（势头）。这种 momentum 形成的声墙淹没了任何新出现的声音，比如“嘿，市场在变。市场在变。”你根本听不到，因为所有 momentum 都朝着一个特定方向。我有一个很好的例子，我在 Google 工作时亲眼目睹。那是 Facebook 崛起的时代。当时 Google 是这样的——人们不记得 2000 年代末和 2010 年代初的 Google。Google 不仅仅是一家公司。它是硅谷的顶级掠食者。苹果呢，MacBook Air 刚刚推出。Steve Jobs 正朝着正确的方向前进，但远非今天的苹果。Amazon AWS 还年轻。Nvidia 濒临破产。我是说，所有你想到的巨头——Microsoft 由 Ballmer 运营，Twitter 还很小。但 Google 已经是超大型的头号公司。每个人都想在 Google 工作。没有多少公司有那样的地位。然后在外围，这家小公司 Facebook 开始崛起。Google 拥有地球上最好的工程师，每月现金流 10 亿美元，试图与这家小公司竞争。我记得当时 Facebook 可能只有一千人。而 Google 的规模是它的 15 到 20 倍，现金流充裕。为什么 Google 打不过 Facebook？因为 Google 不是 Facebook。就像那句儒家名言：大猩猩如何学会飞翔？通过不做大猩猩。Google 赢得社交媒体战争的方式是成为一家社交媒体公司。但它根本就不是。所以，大公司经常发生这种情况：你 consciously 或 unconsciously 沿着一个方向前进，因为所有员工、文化和工作都围绕那个方向。然后市场发生了变化，你根本无法转向。我认为这种情况在小公司中也会惊人地发生。创始人有一个观点，然后那个观点就成了唯一的方向。

transcript.secZh: 优秀人才公司为何失败

transcript.sec: Culture of course correction

turns.en: And actually, that can be just 10° off from what was the correct path and the whole company's kind of led astray. So, they were in the right market. They might have even been solving the right problem, but they were just a little off. And so, we're so scared of failing and so scared of losing that I will humble myself and listen to other people. And they say, 'Hey, we're 5° off course here.' And it's like, 'Okay, let's maybe fix the course.' And then once that becomes your culture, then it's really hard to lose. Because everybody's not about fulfilling a preset path. They're just about finding how to win.
turns.zh: 实际上，可能只是偏离正确路径 10°，整个公司就被带偏了。他们选对了市场，甚至可能解决了正确的问题，但就是差那么一点点。所以我们非常害怕失败、害怕输，我会谦卑下来，倾听别人的意见。他们会说：‘嘿，我们偏了 5°。’然后我就说：‘好，那我们修正一下方向。’一旦这成为你的文化，就很难输了。因为每个人都不是为了完成预设路径，而是为了找到获胜的方法。

turns.en: This is exactly what I wanted to ask about. Everyone listening to this either is like, 'Oh yeah, we're very open-minded. We're absolutely going to listen to everyone's opinions and decide rationally the right path in practice.'
turns.zh: 这正是我想问的。每个听众要么觉得：‘哦，是的，我们非常开放，绝对会听取每个人的意见，理性地决定正确的道路。’

turns.en: Never happens.
turns.zh: 从来不会发生。

turns.en: Right. Or they're just like, 'We're just not good at this. We're just too nice to each other.' How do you do this at a company that isn't good at this? Does it have to be the CEO top-down in your experience? Does it have to be part of the culture? How do you operationalize at a company that's not like you?
turns.zh: 对。或者他们觉得：‘我们就是不擅长这个，我们对彼此太好了。’你如何在一家不擅长此道的公司做到这一点？根据你的经验，这必须由 CEO 自上而下推动吗？必须成为文化的一部分吗？如何在一家不像你这样的公司中落地？

turns.en: You know, the middle way is typically the right way. And it's hard to find the middle way because these are conflicting ideas. These are the guardrails or the flag posts you just set. One side is like, 'We're just going to go.' And the other side is, 'We're too unsure.' And you have to somehow once you do have that debate, you have to then confidently walk down that path. And again, this is conflicting. I just said 'Be humble enough to listen to what's going on.' But then once that decision is made, the decisive... In our values, that first value, the speed value, which is specifically the wording is 'Move fast, move safe.' That's specifically the wording. We assess our managers on adherence to those values. Literally, we compensate and promote against those values. They're not just abstract values. So, the behavior we're actually looking at under speed is decisiveness. So, we're setting a that is looking at these conflicting things. One is like, 'Be open.' And the other one's, 'Make decisions quickly.' And you have to hold those in tension. This is why you as the co-founder or founder of the company get paid the big bucks. You got to do that. You got to know when to bluff and when to hold them and when to fold them, as they say. So, at some point, and that point comes sometimes faster than you think, you will not get any more information and you have to make a decision. So, you're walking this very thin line.
turns.zh: 你知道，中间路线通常是正确的。但找到中间路线很难，因为这些都是相互冲突的想法。这些是你设定的护栏或标杆。一边是‘我们只管往前冲’，另一边是‘我们太不确定了’。你必须以某种方式，在辩论之后，自信地沿着那条路走下去。这又是矛盾的。我刚刚说‘要足够谦卑去倾听’，但一旦做出决定，就要果断……在我们的价值观中，第一个价值观，速度价值观，具体措辞是‘快速行动，安全行动’。就是这个词。我们根据管理者对这些价值观的遵守程度来评估他们。我们真的根据这些价值观来发放薪酬和晋升。它们不是抽象的价值观。所以，我们在速度下真正考察的行为是果断。我们是在审视这些冲突的东西：一个是‘保持开放’，另一个是‘快速决策’。你必须让它们保持张力。这就是为什么你作为公司的联合创始人或创始人能赚大钱。你必须做到这一点。你必须知道什么时候该虚张声势，什么时候该坚持，什么时候该放弃，就像他们说的那样。所以在某个时刻，这个时刻有时比你想象中来得更快，你将得不到更多信息，必须做出决定。所以你是在走一条非常细的线。

turns.en: Your point about emotions was extremely interesting and I want to make sure people don't take away the wrong takeaway here. So, what I actually found really helpful, which I think is aligned with what you were suggesting, is taking emotions out of it. The way I've used this in my work is when you have to make a hard decision, pretend nobody's feelings would be hurt and emotions are not involved. What would you do? If nobody cared, if they're like totally great. So, what would you do in that world? And then that tells you, okay, that's actually the right thing to do. And then it's, okay, how do I help people feel okay about this? How do I deal with the downsides of this path?
turns.zh: 你关于情绪的观点非常有趣，我想确保人们不会误解。我发现非常有帮助的一点是，与你的建议一致，就是把情绪剥离出去。我在工作中使用的方法是：当你必须做出艰难决定时，假装没有人会受伤，情绪不参与。你会怎么做？如果没人介意，一切都很好。那么在那个世界里你会怎么做？然后你就知道，那其实是正确的事。然后就是：我如何帮助人们对此感到安心？我如何处理这条路径的负面影响？

turns.en: Yeah, I think that's the obvious version of it. I think maybe another version is, let's take another route. You as the leader or as the engineer who is getting direction, you already have some preset view that this is my idea. That's an emotional construct, it's around ownership and feeling of ownership. So, yeah, I really fall into that category of like the more... So, even like maybe most fundamentally, what is an emotion? An emotion is like a set of reactions that have been a framework that's been imparted in your brain through life experiences. And those life experiences might not actually make you optimize, they've not been optimized for you to make a decision in a product review. And so, the more you can pull that away, a good heuristic would be the same decision being made by multiple people in the company gets the same result. So, you're removing a little bit of that almost like filter. You can almost think of that emotion as like a filter. So, I like to have the raw image come through, the raw decision come through so we can consistently classify it again and again. Not to get too abstract, but I don't know if that makes sense.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为这是显而易见的版本。也许另一个版本是，我们换一条路。你作为领导者或接受指示的工程师，已经预设了‘这是我的想法’。这是一种情感构造，围绕所有权和所有权感。所以，我确实属于那种更……甚至可能最根本的问题是：什么是情绪？情绪是一系列反应，是通过生活经验植入你大脑的框架。而这些生活经验可能并没有让你优化，它们没有被优化用于在产品评审中做决策。所以，你越能剥离它，一个好的启发式方法是：公司里多个人做出的相同决策会得到相同的结果。这样你就移除了一点那种滤镜。你几乎可以把情绪看作一个滤镜。所以我喜欢让原始图像通过，原始决策通过，这样我们可以反复一致地分类它。不想太抽象，但不知道这有没有道理。

turns.en: Yeah, it makes sense and I have one last question but there's an interesting trend I've noticed with people talking about AGI. The missing piece I've been hearing more and more is just emotions are what creates consciousness potentially. Michael Pollan has a new book out about consciousness and his take is it's not just more intelligence, it's actually emotions that led to the consciousness. I think it's undermining how complex human thought is to think that it's just the inputs and outputs of association of ideas, facts, words, letters. It's not just those associations and creativity is a little bit of that as well. Like with the old saying of technical mastery is mastering the complex and I think computers do that really well and creativity is mastering the simple. I'm sure I'm going to eat my words on this as like the best artist in like three years will be... yeah yeah. And so I think this goes back to my philosophy of consume broad inputs but then try to remove that filter, see things as honestly as they possibly can, create a culture in the company that is also similar and doesn't put any weight on who the idea came from or where it came from but then ultimately as a leader you decide and by the way you got to be right. Like that's the other thing I think a lot of founders that were just not where we don't emphasize enough. Founders love to take credit for things, it's just human nature everybody does. But the reality is you have to be right. It's not enough to just start a company. It's not enough to have this vision of the world. You have to be right. And the evidence is the company a sustainable standalone business. Because we're talking specifically about venture-backed AI companies in Silicon Valley. All of my... I should have said this at the beginning.
turns.zh: 是的，有道理。我还有一个问题，但我注意到一个有趣的趋势，人们在谈论 AGI 时，我越来越多地听到缺失的一块是：情绪可能正是创造意识的东西。迈克尔·波伦出了一本关于意识的新书，他的观点是，不仅仅是更多的智能，实际上是情绪导致了意识。我认为，认为人类思维只是观念、事实、词语、字母的输入输出关联，这低估了人类思维的复杂性。不仅仅是那些关联，创造力也有点像那样。就像老话说的，技术精通是掌握复杂，我认为计算机做得很好，而创造力是掌握简单。我肯定三年后最好的艺术家会让我食言……是的。所以我认为这又回到了我的哲学：广泛吸收输入，然后尝试移除那个滤镜，尽可能诚实地看待事物，在公司里创造类似的文化，不看重想法来自谁或来自哪里，但最终作为领导者你做出决定，而且你必须正确。这是我认为很多创始人没有足够强调的。创始人喜欢把功劳归于自己，这是人性，每个人都这样。但现实是，你必须正确。仅仅创办公司是不够的，仅仅拥有这个愿景也不够。你必须正确。证据就是公司是一个可持续的独立业务。因为我们专门在谈论硅谷的风险投资支持的 AI 公司。我所有的……我一开始就应该说这个。

transcript.secZh: 纠错文化

transcript.sec: Advice for AI founders and the importance of taste

turns.en: All of my advice is specifically for that narrow group, which is founders of AI venture-backed companies in the Bay Area. Speaking of that, last question. I've been wanting to get to this because it's an interesting spicy take that you have. You have this view that a lot of CEOs in Silicon Valley don't actually have great taste. I'm excited to hear about your experience there and what you think about that.
turns.zh: 我所有的建议都专门针对那个狭窄的群体，也就是湾区 AI 风险投资公司的创始人。说到这个，最后一个问题。我一直想问这个，因为你的观点很有趣、很有争议。你认为硅谷很多 CEO 其实品味并不好。我很想听听你的经历和看法。

turns.en: Yeah, I also want to be careful not to imply that I do. I fall into that group. I think it is true for a couple of reasons. Both taste in the most artistic sense and in the most specific sense like running a company and HR policy for point X. A lot of that is they're just not exposed to many interesting good things. That's been a theme in this whole conversation: just get more and more exposure. It's very unfortunate when I meet somebody—and I'm not thinking of anyone particular, so if this is you and you're one of my friends, I apologize, I'm not talking about you—but you grow up in Cupertino, go to Berkeley, and the first thing you do out of school is start a company, and that's all you do for 20 years. You've never even been an employee. Why I think that's so important: I spent over a decade working in large organizations, truly large like more than 100,000 employees, like General Motors or Bosch. When you're in the back alley of that organization, the bowels of those organizations, you learn how bad it is to be an employee. The bureaucracy above you, leadership doesn't know what's going on, the industry, your antiquated tools, all this stuff. Why that's so important to experience as an individual is then when you become a leader, you're making policies and creating culture and you have to keep that in mind. A bunch of founders who never had the fortune of being at the bottom of the totem pole. That's just one version of how to develop taste. It doesn't mean you have to consume the photos of Bresson or Picasso or whoever, but it's something similar. You can sometimes meet founders that would be good at lots of things, not just being a founder of an AI company in the Bay Area. There's something about taste there. Because what you're really talking about is understanding humans and understanding life, and then being able to discern with some judgment what is good and what's not good. If your life experience is very narrow, you could still be good and have the ability to discern, but I think there's something about if you've backpacked for a few years around the world, I somehow believe that's going to be a better founder. I don't know how I can point to peer-reviewed research that says that. So that's what I'm getting at: there is some developing of taste.
turns.zh: 是的，我也想小心别让人觉得我品味很好。我也属于那个群体。我认为这确实是真的，有几个原因。无论是艺术意义上的品味，还是具体到经营公司、制定 HR 政策等方面的品味，很大程度上是因为他们没有接触过很多有趣的好东西。这贯穿了我们整个对话：就是不断接触更多事物。当我遇到一些人——我没有特指谁，所以如果这说的是你，你是我的朋友，我道歉，我不是在说你——你在库比蒂诺长大，上伯克利，毕业后第一件事就是创办公司，然后 20 年只做这一件事。你甚至从未当过员工。为什么我觉得这很重要：我在大型组织工作了十多年，真正的大公司，员工超过 10 万，比如通用汽车或博世。当你身处那些组织的底层，你会明白当员工有多糟糕。上层的官僚主义，领导层不了解情况，行业、过时的工具等等。为什么个人经历这些很重要？因为当你成为领导者时，你要制定政策、创造文化，你必须牢记这些。很多创始人从未有幸处于底层。这只是培养品味的一种方式。这并不意味着你必须看布列松或毕加索的照片，但道理类似。你有时会遇到一些创始人，他们擅长很多事情，而不仅仅是做湾区 AI 公司的创始人。这与品味有关。因为你真正谈论的是理解人类、理解生活，然后能够用判断力辨别什么是好的、什么是不好的。如果你的生活经历非常狭窄，你仍然可能很好，有能力辨别，但我认为如果你花几年时间背包环游世界，我莫名相信你会成为更好的创始人。我无法指出同行评审的研究来证明这一点。所以这就是我的意思：品味是需要培养的。

turns.en: Well, I feel like we have helped people build their taste, feed their model with more insights and different perspectives in this conversation. I feel like we could chat for hours, but I know you got to run.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得我们在这场对话中帮助人们培养了品味，用更多见解和不同视角喂养了他们的模型。我觉得我们可以聊上几个小时，但我知道你得走了。

turns.en: Yeah, I'm not sure if there are any real takeaways other than zero. We really went everywhere. I'm sorry if you had a particular line of questions you wanted to go down. We went in all the perfect directions.
turns.zh: 是的，我不确定除了零之外还有什么真正的收获。我们真的聊得很广。如果你有特定的问题想深入，我很抱歉。我们走遍了所有完美的方向。

turns.en: Good. Qasar, thank you so much for doing this. Thank you so much for being here. Final question, just where can folks find you online? How can listeners be useful to you?
turns.zh: 很好。Qasar，非常感谢你来做这个节目。非常感谢你来到这里。最后一个问题，人们在哪里可以找到你？听众如何能对你有帮助？

turns.en: That's a great question. I mean, I love to hear what books I don't know. So that's always good. Some of my favorite books have been randomly recommended to me. So I'll take that. Of course, I consume research as well. So if there's something particularly novel going on, obviously all the mainstream stuff we as a company and me as an individual are going to consume, but things that are a bit off the beaten path, we're always looking for that. But yeah, and if you have a particular opinion about specifically our domain, physical AI, and how AI is going to impact mines, farms, construction sites, robotics, all of that stuff. I'm always interested to hear new opinions on that or even old opinions maybe with a different viewpoint. So yeah, if you see me, you can see me online. Of course, I'm always around as well. I'm always open to that feedback.
turns.zh: 这是个好问题。我喜欢听我不知道的书。所以那总是好的。我最喜欢的一些书就是被随机推荐的。所以我会接受。当然，我也看研究。所以如果有特别新颖的东西，显然我们公司和我个人会关注所有主流内容，但那些有点冷门的东西，我们一直在寻找。不过，如果你对我们特定的领域——物理 AI，以及 AI 将如何影响矿山、农场、建筑工地、机器人等所有方面——有特别的看法，我总是乐于听取新的观点，甚至可能带有不同视角的旧观点。所以，如果你看到我，你可以在网上找到我。当然，我也一直在。我总是对那种反馈持开放态度。

turns.en: And you're on Twitter now. There you go. Yeah, exactly. Follow me there. So that's the answer. Call to action. Channeling my inner Mark.
turns.zh: 你现在在 Twitter 上。这就对了。是的，没错。在那里关注我。这就是答案。行动号召。模仿我的内心 Mark。

turns.en: There you go. Qasar, thank you so much for doing this and for being here.
turns.zh: 这就对了。Qasar，非常感谢你来做这个节目，感谢你来到这里。

turns.en: Yeah, thanks for having me. It was a lot of fun. Bye, everyone. Thank you so much for listening. If you found this valuable, you can subscribe to the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your favorite podcast app. Also, please consider giving us a rating or leaving a review, as that really helps other listeners find the podcast. You can find all past episodes or learn more about the show at lennyspodcast.com. See you in the next episode.
turns.zh: 是的，谢谢你邀请我。非常有趣。大家再见。非常感谢你的收听。如果你觉得有价值，可以在 Apple Podcasts、Spotify 或你最喜欢的播客应用上订阅本节目。也请考虑给我们评分或留下评论，这真的能帮助其他听众找到这个播客。你可以在 lennyspodcast.com 找到所有过往剧集或了解更多关于节目的信息。下集再见。

transcript.secZh: AI创始人建议与品味重要性
